metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC3 for ADR existence, e.g. `ls docs/adr/*code_review_impact* | grep -q .` or a pytest `test_adr_exists` that asserts the file is present and non-empty.
findings.finding: AC3 includes 'an ADR under docs/adr/ records the model' as a completion criterion but no proving command verifies the ADR file's existence. The pytest commands in AC3 do not check for the presence of any file under `docs/adr/`.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3's proving command: `&& test -n "$(ls docs/adr/*code_review* 2>/dev/null)"` so CI fails if the ADR is missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) that verifies `code-review-verify.md` contains the required 'CODE-REVIEW CONSEQUENCE BINARIES' and 'TRIGGER LIKELIHOOD + DETECTION' blocks — e.g., a snapshot/content test or a grep-based CI check on the prompt file.
findings.finding: The verify-prompt additive block in `code-review-verify.md` is a named described deliverable ('VERIFY-PROMPT INSTRUCTION TEXT' section) but has no covering acceptance criterion — all four ACs verify code/schema/formula artifacts via unit tests that cannot detect a missing or incorrect prompt block.
findings.location: 'VERIFY-PROMPT INSTRUCTION TEXT' section of Implementation mechanics; Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'A snapshot or content-assertion test confirms `code-review-verify.md` contains the additive CONSEQUENCE BINARIES block covering all 10 tier-tagged binaries and the trigger_likelihood/silent_failure/escapes_automation definitions.' Proving command can extend to `pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py tests/unit/test_verify_prompt.py -v`.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) with an end-to-end smoke-test proving command that runs a real code-review gate call (or a recorded-cassette integration test) against a fixture diff and asserts the returned impact score is produced by `impact_code` (e.g. by checking a field value only impact_code emits, such as a lane-decomposition log entry or a score outside impact_plan's range).
findings.finding: The per-gate dispatch cutover (`code_review_decide` passes `impact_fn=impact_code`) is a production default flip, but all proving commands are unit tests (`pytest tests/unit/…`). No AC requires an end-to-end check that exercises the full code-review pipeline — LLM verifier → schema deserialization → DET enrichment injection → impact_code call — as it runs in production. Green unit tests can pass even if the wiring in the YAML gate, the contract registration, or the DET enrichment injection is broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1, AC2, AC3 (all prove via `pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-file churn cache (e.g. `{filepath: churn90}` dict built before the findings loop) so `git log` is invoked at most once per unique file path in `code_review_decide`, eliminating the N-findings-per-file redundant subprocess pattern.
findings.finding: The plan calls `git log` per finding to compute churn_90d, with no mention of deduplicating or caching results across findings that touch the same file. Multiple findings on the same file produce N identical subprocess invocations.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — 'DET ENRICHMENT' section: 'computes churn90 (90-day git commit count for the finding's file) ... per finding'
findings.suggested_fix: Before iterating over findings, collect the set of unique file paths, run one `git log` per path, store results in a dict, then look up rather than re-invoke during the per-finding enrichment loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a debug/warning log line (e.g., `logger.debug('churn fallback: git unavailable for %s, using churn=0', filepath)`) in the git-failure path within `code_review_decide`, so operators can observe when the freq_mult fallback is systematically active.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new failure point (git subprocess for churn_90d) with a specified fallback but no instrumentation — there is no mention of logging, metrics, or any signal when the fallback path is taken, leaving operators blind to systematic git-unavailability or shallow-clone conditions across reviews.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — 'DET ENRICHMENT' + 'CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER' sections; Acceptance Criteria (no observability AC present)
findings.suggested_fix: In the except/fallback branch of the git churn call, emit `logger.warning('code_review_decide: git churn unavailable for file=%s reason=%s; defaulting churn=0', filepath, exc)` so the condition is surfaced in review logs without failing the pipeline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an integration-level test (e.g., `tests/unit/test_code_review_decide.py`) that calls `code_review_decide` (or its enrichment+dispatch sub-routine) with a synthetic finding whose file has known churn and is on a hard-to-reverse surface, and asserts that `impact_code` receives non-zero `churn_90d` and `hard_to_reverse_surface=True` via the verification dict — verifying the injection targets the correct dict, not the finding dict.
findings.finding: The DET enrichment injection path — where `code_review_decide` injects `churn90` / `hard_to_reverse_surface` into `reshape.verifications[i]["severity_attributes"]` (NOT into the finding dict) before calling `pass3_over_findings` — is not covered by any stated test. The unit tests for `impact_code` receive synthetic dicts directly, so they pass even if the injection writes to the wrong target dict (e.g., the finding's own `severity_attributes`). The plan explicitly flags this as a non-obvious correctness concern: 'Writing to the finding's own severity_attributes would silently no-op.' No AC requires a test that exercises the full `code_review_decide` → enrichment-injection → `pass3_over_findings` → `impact_code` chain.
findings.location: AC1 / AC2 / Implementation mechanics — 'DET ENRICHMENT (merge target = the VERIFICATION dict, NOT the finding dict)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one test that constructs a minimal `reshape` object with a finding pointing at a known file, mocks `git log` to return a fixed commit count, calls the enrichment step, then calls `impact_code` with the resulting verification dict and asserts `freq_mult > 0.5` and (for a migration path) `reversibility_floor = 0.6`. This directly exercises the dict-targeting logic the plan identifies as the key correctness hazard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py` (line 142) to the Scope: update the assertion from `"outputs: verification"` to `"outputs: code_review_verification"` when the front-matter rename lands.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py` line 142 contains the assertion `assert "outputs: verification" in body` which pins the exact front-matter value of `code-review-verify.md`. The plan changes that file's `outputs:` key from `verification` to `code_review_verification`, which will cause this existing test to fail. This test file is not listed in the plan's Scope as a target to update.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics section (three named wiring places); `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py` line 142
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 142 to: `assert "outputs: code_review_verification" in body  # code-review-specific contract`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the old baseline function (module + function name) used as the comparison scalar in AC4, or state the numeric gap value it produces on the fixture so the test can assert against a constant.
findings.finding: AC4 requires that `impact_code`'s median gap on the labeled fixture is 'strictly GREATER than the old mean `impact`'s gap on the same fixture', but the plan never names the old baseline function, its module path, or how to invoke it inside the test. An executor writing `test_impact_code.py` cannot implement this comparison clause without guessing which prior function to call.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC4 (last bullet); Implementation mechanics / calibration section
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to AC4: 'Baseline = `decide.impact` (the current shared function, pre-patch) applied to each fixture row using its existing `severity_attributes`; import it as `from rebar.llm.review_kernel.decide import impact as impact_legacy`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a feature flag to fall back to the old impact path, or an expand-contract deployment sequence) so the old impact behavior can be restored without a full redeploy if the new formula mis-scores in production.
findings.finding: The plan re-anchors the impact model for all code-review findings (drops the 0.33 attribute floor, replaces the old mean-based scalar with the two-lane MAX formula) with no rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequencing if the new formula mis-scores findings in a live review session.
findings.location: 'RE-ANCHOR' bullet in 'What' section; Acceptance Criteria AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract all calibration-sensitive constants (tier values, trigger_likelihood multipliers, freq_mult parameters, reversibility_floor, amp values) and the hard-to-reverse surface rules into a named constants block or config structure (e.g., a `CODE_REVIEW_IMPACT_CONFIG` dataclass or module-level dict) so the calibration child can update them in one place without hunting magic literals across decide.py.
findings.finding: All calibration-sensitive thresholds and rules are hardcoded in decide.py with no externalization mechanism, despite a dedicated calibration child ticket that will need to change them.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `CODE_REVIEW_IMPACT_PARAMS` dataclass or named tuple at the top of `decide.py` (or a sibling `impact_code_config.py`) holding all scalar constants and the surface-rule sets. `impact_code` reads from this struct. The calibration child ticket then has a single, auditable diff target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before or as part of the rename, grep for references to `verification` (as the code-review output schema key) in CLAUDE.md, README files, and any developer/integration documentation, and update or note that only the code-review gate's schema is renamed while the plan-review kernel's `verification` contract is unchanged.
findings.finding: The plan renames the code-review output schema from `verification` to `code_review_verification` in three wiring points but does not audit whether any existing developer documentation (CLAUDE.md, README, integration guides) references the old `verification` key for the code-review gate, potentially stranding stale references.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics: 'THREE named places' — code_review/contracts.py, code-review.yaml (~line 147), code-review-verify.md outputs key

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly acknowledge and update (or confirm no-change-needed for) the reshape/normalization layer that maps gate output keys to `verifs[i]`, so the rename from `verification` to `code_review_verification` in code-review.yaml and code-review-verify.md does not silently empty the verifications dict at runtime.
findings.finding: The plan changes the gate output schema key from `verification` to `code_review_verification` in two files (code-review.yaml, code-review-verify.md) but does not acknowledge whether the reshape layer (which populates `reshape.verifications[i]` that `pass3_decide` ultimately reads) needs updating to map the new key name. If reshape reads the gate's output dict by the literal key `verification`, the rename silently empties the verifications dict for every code-review finding.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — 'Wired in THREE named places' and DET enrichment paragraph; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope and Implementation mechanics: identify the code path that reads the gate's raw output dict and populates reshape.verifications[i] (e.g., a key lookup or schema dispatch in reshape.py or the workflow runner), and either (a) confirm it already uses the contract name dynamically (so any contract key works), or (b) add it as a named change target alongside the three already listed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/__init__.py` to the Scope and task list: export `impact_code` from `.decide` (mirroring the existing `impact_plan` export on line 49).
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/__init__.py` is not listed in the plan's Scope but must export `impact_code` once it is added to `decide.py` — the module follows the convention of re-exporting every public symbol from `decide` (confirmed: `impact`, `impact_plan`, `pass3_decide`, etc. are all exported), and `code_review_decide` imports from `rebar.llm import review_kernel`.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, append `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/__init__.py` (add `impact_code` to the `__all__` export list and the `from .decide import ...` line).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art citation or empirical rationale for the freq_mult formula constants (0.5 floor, 30-commit saturation, 90-day window) — e.g. reference defect-prediction literature, SonarQube hotspot / CodeClimate churn signals, or the dogfood distribution that drove these specific values — so the formula shape is a grounded design choice rather than an arbitrary commitment.
findings.finding: The freq_mult formula (freq_mult = 0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30) introduces a novel churn-weighted scoring sub-component with no prior-art reference or justification for its specific constants — the 0.5 floor, 30-commit saturation, and 90-day window are committed design choices with direct calibration consequences, yet no established prior art (e.g., defect-prediction literature, SonarQube hotspot metric, CodeClimate churn signal) is cited to justify these specific values or the linear ramp shape over alternatives (e.g., log scale, different saturation thresholds).
findings.location: What section — CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER: 'freq_mult = 0.5 + 0.5 * min(churn_90d, 30) / 30 where churn_90d = commits touching the finding's file in the last 90 days'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER section, add a one-sentence rationale such as: 'Constants chosen from the dogfood corpus: median active file = ~15 commits/90d, so saturation at 30 (2× median) lets the multiplier discriminate without capping too early; floor of 0.5 ensures maint findings on cold files are not zeroed. Revisit via the calibration child if the fixture shows floor/saturation are wrong.' Alternatively cite a prior-art source (e.g., Mockus & Weiss 2000 defect-prediction churn correlation) for the linear shape.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `git log` flag surface for `churn_90d`: state the date flag (`--since='90 days ago'`), whether `--follow` is used for rename tracking, and the counting approach (e.g., `--oneline -- <path> | wc -l` or `--format=%H`); also distinguish the shallow-clone case (where git succeeds but history is truncated) from the 'git unavailable' case so the correct fallback fires.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `git log` (DET) to compute `churn_90d` but does not name the exact flag surface, leaving the implementor to guess among several non-equivalent approaches: `--since='90 days ago'` vs `--after`, with or without `--follow` (renames silently return zero without it), and with no stated counting method (`--oneline | wc -l` vs `--format=%H`). The shallow-clone fallback is named ('churn_90d=0') but `git log --since=...` on a shallow clone silently underreports rather than failing, so the stated fallback condition ('git unavailable') does not cover it.
findings.location: Implementation mechanics — CHANGE-FREQUENCY MULTIPLIER / DET ENRICHMENT paragraphs
findings.suggested_fix: Document the canonical invocation in the plan: `git log --since='90 days ago' --follow --oneline -- <path>` piped through line-count, with a separate shallow-clone probe (`git rev-parse --is-shallow-repository`) that routes to the churn_90d=0 fallback when the repo is shallow.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
